title: Roast venison haunch
subtitle: Medium-rare, rested, with a red wine and juniper sauce
excerpt: A joint of venison roasted hot and fast to medium-rare, rested for 20 minutes, and served with a dark red wine sauce made from the roasting tin. Lean, gamey, and deeply satisfying.
sourceNotes: Classic British game roast. Venison is best served medium-rare as it dries out quickly above 60 degrees C internal temperature.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate the joint overnight in the fridge in the red wine, garlic, juniper, and herbs for a deeper flavour.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven for the initial sear; 180 degrees C fan for the main roast

glossaryTerms.definition: Soaking meat in an acidic or seasoned liquid before cooking to add flavour and, in the case of game, soften the sometimes intense taste.
Leaving cooked meat to stand before carving. Venison is particularly important to rest; the juices distribute unevenly if carved immediately.

content.text: Venison is the leanest of all roasting meats and the one most commonly overcooked. It needs to be served pink: 55 to 60 degrees C at the centre, carved in slices, and never pushed above well-done. The dark red sauce is built from the roasting tin while the joint rests.
the joint overnight in the olive oil, red wine, juniper, thyme, and garlic in the fridge. Remove from the marinade and pat dry before cooking.

Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Rub the joint all over with olive oil, salt, and crushed juniper. Roast at 220 degrees C for 15 minutes to colour the outside, then reduce to 180 degrees C and continue for 30 to 35 minutes until the internal temperature reads 55 to 60 degrees C for medium-rare.
Transfer to a warm board and
for 20 minutes under a tent of foil.

Pour off most of the fat from the roasting tin. Set over medium heat and add the red wine, scraping up any sticky residue. Reduce by half. Add the hot stock and simmer for 8 minutes. Remove from the heat and whisk in the cold butter cubes for a glossy finish. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve into a warm jug.

items.symptom: The venison is overcooked and grey throughout
items.cause: The joint was taken to an internal temperature above 65 degrees C.
items.fix: Venison cooks very quickly. Check with a thermometer from 35 minutes onwards and pull at exactly 55-58 degrees C. It will rise 3-5 degrees C during resting.

items.symptom: The sauce is thin and tastes watery
items.cause: Not enough reduction after the stock was added.
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for longer. The butter finish adds body and gloss as well as flavour.

items.symptom: The exterior is pale after the initial blast
items.cause: The joint was very cold when it went in, or the oven was not preheated to a true 220 degrees C.
items.fix: Always bring venison to room temperature for 30 minutes. The high blast must happen with a properly preheated oven.

items.symptom: The meat has a very strong gamey flavour
items.cause: Wild deer that was not properly hung, or a buck rather than a hind.
items.fix: An overnight marinade softens the flavour. Ask the butcher whether the animal was properly hung for at least 5 to 7 days.

content.text: A chocolate and red wine sauce works well with venison: add 30 g of dark chocolate to the sauce in place of the butter. For a fruity alternative, reduce a jar of redcurrant jelly with the wine before adding the stock.

Venison has been a British game meat since before the Norman conquest. The medieval forest laws that restricted hunting to the nobility made venison a prestige food for centuries. It fell out of everyday British cooking after the Second World War. Venison has only been widely available in supermarkets since the 1990s, when Scottish deer farming expanded.
